Metadata.Coram: Tay Yong Kwang JA, Steven Chong JA and Quentin Loh J
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: The Wellness Group Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: The Wellness Group Pte Ltd incorporated TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd as its wholly-owned subsidiary in or around October 2007.
By 2010, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd held 84.2% of the shares in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, after Paris Investment Pte Ltd acquired 15.8% of the shares.

On 18 March 2011, a sale and purchase agreement was signed under which OSIM International Pte Ltd purchased a 35% stake in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd from The Wellness Group Pte Ltd and Paris Investment Pte Ltd.

On 18 March 2011, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, OSIM International Pte Ltd, Paris Investment Pte Ltd and TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd signed a Shareholders' Agreement containing clause 5 on the composition of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd's Board of Directors.

Following the 18 March 2011 sale and purchase, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, OSIM International Pte Ltd and Paris Investment Pte Ltd held 54.7%, 35% and 10.3% of the shares in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd respectively.

Clause 5.2.1 of the 18 March 2011 Shareholders' Agreement provided that two persons on the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd would be appointed by Paris Investment Pte Ltd and The Wellness Group Pte Ltd.

Clause 5.2.2 of the 18 March 2011 Shareholders' Agreement provided that one person on the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd would be appointed by OSIM International Pte Ltd, for so long as OSIM International Pte Ltd's shareholding percentage was not less than 25%, and specified that this person would be Mr Ron Sim.
From 18 March 2011, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd was entitled under the Shareholders' Agreement, together with Paris Investment Pte Ltd, to appoint two persons to the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd.

From 18 March 2011, OSIM International Pte Ltd was entitled under the Shareholders' Agreement to appoint one specified person, Mr Ron Sim, to the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd while its shareholding remained at or above 25%.

Following a rights issue conducted between 2013 and 2014, the shareholdings in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd changed to 30.1% for The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, 58.6% for OSIM International Pte Ltd and 11.3% for Paris Investment Pte Ltd.

On 28 September 2012, Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani resigned from the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, after having previously served as a director, and The Wellness Group Pte Ltd did not appoint another director in his place immediately thereafter.

In February 2014, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd and its chairman initiated a minority oppression action in court against OSIM International Pte Ltd, Paris Investment Pte Ltd and directors of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd.
By February 2014, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd held a minority shareholding in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd relative to OSIM International Pte Ltd.
On 26 October 2016, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd sought to have Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani appointed to the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd pursuant to its contractual entitlement to representation.
On 26 October 2016, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd communicated to TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd its proposal to appoint Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani to the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd.

On 13 February 2017, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd wrote to TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd proposing that Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen be appointed to the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd instead of Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani.

In its 13 February 2017 letter, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd requested that the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd authorise Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen to disclose to The Wellness Group Pte Ltd information relating to TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd that he would access as a director, in accordance with section 158 of the Companies Act.

In the same 13 February 2017 letter, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd requested that TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd arrange director and officer insurance coverage for Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen to the same extent as for other directors, or procure such insurance if it was not already in place.

On 13 February 2017, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd asked TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd to formalise as soon as practicable the appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen as a director together with the requested ancillary arrangements.

On 17 February 2017, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd wrote again to TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd requesting that it immediately take all necessary steps to formalise the appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen, including the requested ancillary arrangements.

On 21 February 2017, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd wrote to TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd demanding the formalisation of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen’s appointment as a director, without referring to the previously mentioned ancillary arrangements.

On 23 February 2017, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd replied to The Wellness Group Pte Ltd stating that it would not appoint Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen because the Board was unable to agree to the requested ancillary arrangements and considered them not in the company’s interests.

On 27 February 2017, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd filed Originating Summons No 206 of 2017 in the High Court seeking court orders relating to its entitlement to appoint one director to the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd and the appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen.

As at 27 February 2017, the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd comprised Mr Taha Bouqdib, appointed by OSIM International Pte Ltd, and Mr Ron Sim Chye Hock, appointed by Paris Investment Pte Ltd, and The Wellness Group Pte Ltd had no representative on the Board since 28 September 2012.

On 12 July 2017, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd wrote to TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, copying OSIM International Pte Ltd and Paris Investment Pte Ltd, stating that it appointed Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen to the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd with immediate effect and requesting that TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd take steps to formalise his appointment and update its register of directors.
On 19 July 2017, Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen provided written confirmation to TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd that his appointment was not contingent on the previously requested ancillary arrangements.

On 20 July 2017, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd provided written confirmation to TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd that its appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen was not contingent on the previously requested ancillary arrangements.

On 21 July 2017, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd informed The Wellness Group Pte Ltd that its Board would convene a meeting as soon as practicable to consider the nomination of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen and stated that it disagreed that The Wellness Group Pte Ltd had an absolute and unfettered right to appoint any person it wished.
On 3 August 2017, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd informed The Wellness Group Pte Ltd that its Board would meet on 7 August 2017 to discuss the appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen.

On 8 August 2017, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd wrote to The Wellness Group Pte Ltd stating that the Board considered that Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen did not appear to have substantive commercial experience, particularly in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s industry, and requested an explanation of how his experience and qualifications were relevant and would add value to the Board.
On 10 August 2017, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd filed a Notice of Appeal against the High Court’s decision in Originating Summons No 206 of 2017.

On 17 August 2017, OSIM International Pte Ltd and Paris Investment Pte Ltd wrote to The Wellness Group Pte Ltd stating that, based on their review, they considered that Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen did not appear to have relevant commercial experience and qualifications to add value to TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s business, and invited further details of his experience and an explanation of how it would add value.

On 20 September 2017, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd wrote to TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd stating that it saw no basis for refusing the appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen and that it did not consider itself obliged to justify or explain its choice of him as nominee.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, under the shareholders' contractual arrangements and company constitution, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd’s pre-dispute right regarding Board representation in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd operates as (a) a power to select and require appointment of one individual to the Board whenever it holds at least 25% of the shares, or (b) only a right to propose a candidate the Board’s discretionary decision based on company interests.
Party_Details.Rule: Where shareholders enter into a binding agreement allocating Board seats by reference to shareholdings, courts generally interpret such provisions to give practical effect to minority representation while remaining consistent with statutory director qualification requirements and the Board’s obligation to act in the company’s interests. Contractual rights to Board representation can be specifically enforced, and company constitutions and shareholders’ agreements are read together so that the Board’s formal appointment power is exercised in line with agreed nomination or appointment rights, unless doing so would require appointing a person who is legally disqualified or whose appointment would clearly harm the company.
Party_Details.Application: The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, OSIM International Pte Ltd, Paris Investment Pte Ltd and TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd executed a Shareholders' Agreement on 2011-03-18 that fixed Board composition and expressly tied one Board seat to OSIM International Pte Ltd while allocating two seats to Paris Investment Pte Ltd and The Wellness Group Pte Ltd collectively. After a rights issue between 2013 and 2014, The Wellness Group Pte Ltd’s shareholding decreased and OSIM International Pte Ltd’s shareholding increased, but the contractual Board allocation remained unchanged. The Wellness Group Pte Ltd did not have a representative on the Board after 2012-09-28 and later sought to have first Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani and then Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen placed on the Board. The Wellness Group Pte Ltd sent multiple written communications in February 2017 and July 2017 requesting that TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd formalise the appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen, and confirmed in July 2017 that his appointment was not contingent on additional information-sharing and insurance arrangements. TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd and the other shareholders responded that they would consider the nomination, requested confirmations and further information, and expressed views about Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen’s commercial experience, while not completing his appointment before the dispute escalated into court proceedings. These events frame the pre-dispute interaction between the contractual Board seat allocation, the Board’s formal appointment power under the constitution, and the parties’ differing views on how far The Wellness Group Pte Ltd’s selection of a nominee constrained the Board’s discretion.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The Court of Appeal analysed the High Court’s earlier implication of a term into the Shareholders’ Agreement that allocated two Board seats to the majority shareholder(s) and one Board seat to the minority shareholder(s) holding at least 25% of the shares. It considered the competing characterisations of this implied term: Wellness argued that it conferred an unfettered right to appoint a director whose status arose immediately upon nomination, while the respondents argued that it conferred only a right to nominate, leaving the Board with full discretion to accept or reject the nominee based on company interests. The Court examined the commercial purpose of the Shareholders’ Agreement and the implied term, emphasising that the arrangement was intended to secure effective minority representation on the Board and would be undermined if the Board could indefinitely delay or refuse appointments by demanding justification of nominees. At the same time, the Court rejected the notion that mere nomination automatically made a person a director, noting practical and legal difficulties, including uncertainty over the person’s authority, remuneration, and public notice, and clarifying that the concept of a de facto director did not apply to a nominee who had not yet acted as a director or been held out as such.

The Court drew on British Murac Syndicate, Limited v Alperton Rubber Company, Limited and Plantations Trust Limited v Bila (Sumatra) Rubber Lands Limited, accepting that contractual rights to Board representation can be more than a bare right to nominate and can be specifically enforced, but declining to follow British Murac to the extent it treated nomination alone as constituting appointment. It endorsed the idea, reflected in both British Murac and Plantations Trust, that a company need not accept a nominee who is unfit or whose appointment would be injurious, and reformulated this as a limitation built into the implied term itself: the Board is obliged to appoint the minority’s nominee unless the nominee is statutorily disqualified, does not consent to act, is obviously unfit for office, or would be obviously injurious to the company. The burden lies on the Board to demonstrate such unsuitability; it is not sufficient merely to assert that the nominee lacks particular experience or may disagree with existing directors.

The Court reconciled the implied term with Article 91 of TWG’s Constitution, which vests the power to appoint directors in the Board. It held that the implied term does not transfer the appointment power away from the Board but constrains how the Board must exercise that power in light of the shareholders’ agreement. The Board must generally give effect to the minority shareholder’s choice, subject to the limited exceptions, thereby aligning the contractual allocation of Board seats with the Board’s fiduciary obligation to act in the company’s interests. The Court rejected arguments that this arrangement unlawfully usurped the Board’s powers, pointing to clause 12 of the Shareholders’ Agreement, which expressly provides that the agreement prevails over the Constitution as between the shareholders, and to authorities recognising the enforceability of shareholders’ agreements that regulate how powers conferred by the articles are to be exercised.

On the evidential issues, the Court partially allowed Wellness’s application to adduce further evidence on appeal, admitting the post-hearing correspondence but excluding the Post-Hearing Information Pack (PHIP). It held that the PHIP, prepared to comply with Hong Kong listing requirements, did not materially illuminate the respondents’ motives regarding the nomination and would not have a perceptible impact on the appeal. The Court noted that Wellness had not alleged bad faith in the High Court and that it would be inappropriate to make findings of improper motive based solely on documentary material without cross-examination.

Turning to breach, the Court rejected the respondents’ contention that Wellness’s cause of action was confined to the Board’s refusal to accept the ancillary information-sharing and insurance requests. It observed that the Originating Summons sought only the appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen and did not seek orders on the ancillary matters, and that Wellness’s supporting affidavit framed the dispute as the Board’s refusal to appoint the nominee. The Court concluded that the alleged breach concerned the failure to appoint the nominee, not the rejection of the ancillary requests. It also rejected the respondents’ assertion that they had not yet decided on the appointment, noting that they had refused to appoint the nominee in February 2017 on the basis of the ancillary matters and, after Wellness and the nominee confirmed that the appointment was not contingent on those matters, continued to delay by requesting further information and expressing reservations about the nominee’s experience. The Court held that the Board was not entitled to postpone appointment by placing the onus on Wellness to justify its choice and that, in the absence of evidence that the nominee was statutorily disqualified, unwilling to act, obviously unfit, or obviously injurious to the company, the respondents’ refusal to appoint him amounted to a breach of the implied term.

On remedy, the Court considered whether specific performance was appropriate. It distinguished Plantations Trust, explaining that the refusal of specific performance in that case turned on the plaintiff’s failure to perform its own financing obligations and the resulting inequity of enforcing the appointment right, rather than on any absolute rule against specific performance of director-appointment contracts. The Court referred to commentary and case law indicating that contractual rights to appoint directors, particularly where designed to protect shareholder interests in a joint venture, may be specifically enforced, subject to concerns about unfit nominees and public policy. It rejected the argument that the obligation was a non-enforceable contract for services, noting that the core obligation was to give effect to a structural governance arrangement rather than to compel personal performance of managerial duties. The Court concluded that damages would be inadequate to compensate for the loss of Board representation and that, in principle, specific performance was an appropriate remedy to enforce the implied term, while still recognising that the Board retained a narrow residual ability to resist appointment if it could substantiate one of the recognised exceptions.

Ultimately, the Court dismissed the appeal on the specific relief sought in the Originating Summons, while clarifying the nature of the implied term, the Board’s obligations under it, and the circumstances in which a minority shareholder’s contractual right to Board representation can be enforced. It set out that the Board must appoint the minority’s nominee unless it can demonstrate statutory disqualification, lack of consent, obvious unfitness, or obvious injury to the company, and that mere disagreement with the nominee’s perceived commercial experience or anticipated voting behaviour is insufficient to justify refusal.

Metadata.Coram: Tay Yong Kwang JA, Steven Chong JA and Quentin Loh J
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Paris Investment Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: In 2010, Paris Investment Pte Ltd acquired 15.8% of the shares in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, with The Wellness Group Pte Ltd holding the remaining 84.2% at that time.
Paris Investment Pte Ltd was, by 2010, a shareholder of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd and was wholly owned by OSIM International Pte Ltd.

On 18 March 2011, Paris Investment Pte Ltd, together with The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, OSIM International Pte Ltd and TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, entered into a Shareholders' Agreement governing the affairs of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd and the parties' rights as shareholders.
Following the 18 March 2011 sale and purchase transaction, Paris Investment Pte Ltd held 10.3% of the shares in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd.

Under clause 5.2.1 of the 18 March 2011 Shareholders' Agreement, Paris Investment Pte Ltd, together with The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, was entitled to have two persons appointed to the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd.
After a rights issue conducted between 2013 and 2014, Paris Investment Pte Ltd’s shareholding in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd became 11.3%.

Following the 2013–2014 rights issue, Paris Investment Pte Ltd and The Wellness Group Pte Ltd together held 41.4% of the shares in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, while OSIM International Pte Ltd held 58.6%.

Despite OSIM International Pte Ltd becoming the majority shareholder after the 2013–2014 rights issue, the Board structure under clause 5 of the Shareholders' Agreement continued to allocate two Board seats to Paris Investment Pte Ltd and The Wellness Group Pte Ltd collectively and one Board seat to OSIM International Pte Ltd.
As at 27 February 2017, Paris Investment Pte Ltd’s nominee, Mr Ron Sim Chye Hock, sat on the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd.

On 26 October 2016, Paris Investment Pte Ltd, together with OSIM International Pte Ltd and TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, declined to proceed with the appointment of Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani to the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, and suggested instead that The Wellness Group Pte Ltd consider appointing either Ms Kanchan Murjani or Mr Finian Tan, both of whom were directors of The Wellness Group Pte Ltd.

On 23 February 2017, Paris Investment Pte Ltd, through TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s response, participated in the position that TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd would not appoint Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen because the Board did not agree to the requested ancillary information and insurance arrangements.

On 18 July 2017, Paris Investment Pte Ltd wrote to The Wellness Group Pte Ltd stating that it required written confirmation that the appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen was not contingent on the ancillary arrangements and that, upon such confirmation, it would consider the request to appoint him having regard to whether the appointment would be in the interests of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd.

On 24 July 2017, Paris Investment Pte Ltd wrote to The Wellness Group Pte Ltd stating that it disagreed that The Wellness Group Pte Ltd had an absolute and unfettered right to appoint any person it wished to the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd and that the Board would consider the nomination of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen.

On 17 August 2017, Paris Investment Pte Ltd, together with OSIM International Pte Ltd, wrote to The Wellness Group Pte Ltd stating that, based on their review, they considered that Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen did not appear to have relevant commercial experience and qualifications to add value to TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s business and invited further details of his experience and an explanation of how it would add value.

Party_Details.Issue: How Paris Investment Pte Ltd’s position as a shareholder and nominating party in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd interacts with the contractual Board seat allocation and the Board’s formal appointment power when The Wellness Group Pte Ltd selects a nominee for the minority-designated Board seat.
Party_Details.Rule: In joint venture and shareholder arrangements, a shareholder that is party to a shareholders’ agreement allocating Board seats is generally participate in giving effect to agreed nomination or appointment rights, while also considering the company’s interests and any statutory constraints on director appointments. Where the agreement states that it prevails over the constitution as between shareholders, the shareholders’ voting and decision-making in relation to Board composition are ordinarily guided by that agreement, applicable company law.
Party_Details.Application: Paris Investment Pte Ltd became a shareholder of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd in 2010 and, together with its parent OSIM International Pte Ltd and The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, entered into the 2011 Shareholders’ Agreement that fixed Board composition. After the 2013–2014 rights issue, Paris Investment Pte Ltd’s shareholding remained a minority stake, while its nominee, Mr Ron Sim Chye Hock, continued to sit on the Board. When The Wellness Group Pte Ltd later sought to have first Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani and then Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen placed on the Board, Paris Investment Pte Ltd participated in communications indicating that it did not support the initial proposal of Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani and that it would only consider the later nomination of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen after receiving confirmations and further information. These pre-dispute communications show Paris Investment Pte Ltd acting in its capacity as a shareholder and as the nominating party for one of the existing directors, within the framework of the Shareholders’ Agreement and the company’s constitution.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The Court treated Paris Investment Pte Ltd, as a wholly owned subsidiary of OSIM International Pte Ltd and a party to the Shareholders’ Agreement, as part of the group of respondents whose conduct in relation to the nomination and appointment of a director by The Wellness Group Pte Ltd was assessed collectively. It noted that Paris Investment Pte Ltd held a minority shareholding after the 2013–2014 rights issue but, together with The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, was allocated two Board seats under clause 5 of the Shareholders’ Agreement, while OSIM International Pte Ltd held the majority of the shares and one Board seat. The Court considered Paris Investment Pte Ltd’s role in the refusal to accept the initial proposal of Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani and in the subsequent correspondence regarding Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen, including letters that requested confirmations about ancillary arrangements, expressed disagreement with the notion of an unfettered appointment right, and questioned the nominee’s commercial experience.

In its legal analysis, the Court did not isolate Paris Investment Pte Ltd’s obligations from those of OSIM International Pte Ltd and TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, but instead focused on the collective obligation arising from the implied term in the Shareholders’ Agreement and the Board’s appointment power under the constitution. It held that the implied term required the Board to appoint the minority shareholder’s nominee unless specific exceptions applied, and that the respondents, including Paris Investment Pte Ltd, had not demonstrated that any such exception applied to Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen. The Court concluded that the respondents’ refusal to appoint the nominee, and their continued delay after the ancillary arrangements were disavowed, amounted to a breach of the implied term. However, the Court ultimately dismissed the appeal on the particular relief sought, while clarifying the legal framework governing the interaction between the shareholders’ agreement, the constitution, and the Board’s appointment decisions.

Metadata.Coram: Tay Yong Kwang JA, Steven Chong JA and Quentin Loh J
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: OSIM International Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: By 2010, OSIM International Pte Ltd wholly owned Paris Investment Pte Ltd.

On 18 March 2011, OSIM International Pte Ltd entered into a sale and purchase agreement under which it purchased a 35% stake in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd from The Wellness Group Pte Ltd and Paris Investment Pte Ltd.

On 18 March 2011, OSIM International Pte Ltd, together with The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, Paris Investment Pte Ltd and TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, signed a Shareholders' Agreement that included clause 5 on the composition of the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd.
Following the 18 March 2011 sale and purchase, OSIM International Pte Ltd held 35% of the shares in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd.

Under clause 5.2.2 of the 18 March 2011 Shareholders' Agreement, OSIM International Pte Ltd was entitled to have one person, specified as Mr Ron Sim, appointed to the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd for so long as its shareholding percentage remained at or above 25%.
After a rights issue conducted between 2013 and 2014, OSIM International Pte Ltd’s shareholding in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd increased to 58.6%.

Following the 2013–2014 rights issue, OSIM International Pte Ltd became the majority shareholder of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd while continuing to be entitled under the Shareholders' Agreement to appoint one director to the Board.
As at 27 February 2017, OSIM International Pte Ltd’s nominee, Mr Taha Bouqdib, sat on the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd.

In February 2014, OSIM International Pte Ltd was named as a defendant in a minority oppression action initiated by The Wellness Group Pte Ltd and its chairman concerning the affairs of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd.

On 26 October 2016, OSIM International Pte Ltd, together with Paris Investment Pte Ltd and TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, declined to proceed with the appointment of Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani to the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd and suggested that The Wellness Group Pte Ltd consider appointing either Ms Kanchan Murjani or Mr Finian Tan instead.

On 23 February 2017, OSIM International Pte Ltd, through TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s response, participated in the position that TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd would not appoint Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen because the Board did not agree to the requested ancillary information and insurance arrangements.

On 18 July 2017, OSIM International Pte Ltd wrote to The Wellness Group Pte Ltd stating that it required written confirmation that the appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen was not contingent on the ancillary arrangements and that, upon such confirmation, it would consider the request to appoint him having regard to whether the appointment would be in the interests of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd.

On 24 July 2017, OSIM International Pte Ltd wrote to The Wellness Group Pte Ltd stating that it disagreed that The Wellness Group Pte Ltd had an absolute and unfettered right to appoint any person it wished to the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd and that the Board would consider the nomination of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen.

On 17 August 2017, OSIM International Pte Ltd, together with Paris Investment Pte Ltd, wrote to The Wellness Group Pte Ltd stating that, based on their review, they considered that Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen did not appear to have relevant commercial experience and qualifications to add value to TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s business and invited further details of his experience and an explanation of how it would add value.

Party_Details.Issue: How OSIM International Pte Ltd’s majority shareholding and contractual entitlement to one Board seat in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd interact with The Wellness Group Pte Ltd’s contractual right to Board representation and the Board’s formal appointment power when a minority nominee is proposed.
Party_Details.Rule: A majority shareholder that is party to a shareholders’ agreement allocating Board seats is generally exercise its influence and voting rights consistently with that agreement, while also ensuring that any director appointments comply with statutory qualification requirements and the Board’s obligation to act in the company’s interests. The majority’s control position does not, by itself, negate a minority shareholder’s contractually agreed right to Board representation.
Party_Details.Application: OSIM International Pte Ltd acquired a 35% stake in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd in 2011 and, after the 2013–2014 rights issue, held 58.6% of the shares, making it the majority shareholder. Under the 2011 Shareholders’ Agreement, OSIM International Pte Ltd was allocated one Board seat, while two seats were allocated to Paris Investment Pte Ltd and The Wellness Group Pte Ltd collectively. When The Wellness Group Pte Ltd later proposed first Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani and then Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen as its Board representative, OSIM International Pte Ltd participated in the responses that declined the initial proposal and conditioned consideration of the later nomination on confirmations and further information. These actions occurred against the backdrop of OSIM International Pte Ltd’s majority shareholding and its existing Board representation, and formed part of the pre-dispute pattern of how the majority shareholder engaged with the minority’s attempt to fill its designated Board seat under the contractual framework.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The Court considered OSIM International Pte Ltd’s role as the majority shareholder and as a party to the Shareholders’ Agreement that structured the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd. It noted that OSIM International Pte Ltd’s shareholding increased to 58.6% after the rights issue, while the Board allocation under clause 5 of the Shareholders’ Agreement continued to provide OSIM International Pte Ltd with one Board seat and Paris Investment Pte Ltd and The Wellness Group Pte Ltd collectively with two seats. The Court observed that this produced a situation in which the majority shareholder had fewer contractually allocated Board seats than the combined minority shareholdings, and that this arrangement was addressed in earlier litigation through the implication of a term aligning Board seats with majority and minority shareholdings.

In assessing the dispute over the appointment of a director nominated by The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, the Court treated OSIM International Pte Ltd, Paris Investment Pte Ltd and TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd as a group of respondents whose conduct was to be evaluated in light of the implied term and the Board’s appointment power. It reviewed OSIM International Pte Ltd’s participation in declining the initial proposal of Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani and in the subsequent correspondence regarding Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen, including letters that requested confirmations about ancillary arrangements, expressed disagreement with the notion of an unfettered appointment right, and questioned the nominee’s commercial experience. The Court concluded that the respondents, including OSIM International Pte Ltd, had not shown that any of the recognised exceptions to the obligation to appoint the minority’s nominee applied, and that their refusal and delay in appointing the nominee constituted a breach of the implied term. Nonetheless, the Court dismissed the appeal on the specific relief sought, while clarifying the legal principles governing the interaction between majority control, contractual Board seat allocation, and the Board’s formal appointment decisions.

Metadata.Coram: Tay Yong Kwang JA, Steven Chong JA and Quentin Loh J
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd was incorporated in or around October 2007 as a wholly-owned subsidiary of The Wellness Group Pte Ltd.
By 2010, the shares in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd were held 84.2% by The Wellness Group Pte Ltd and 15.8% by Paris Investment Pte Ltd.

On 18 March 2011, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd became a party to a Shareholders' Agreement with The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, Paris Investment Pte Ltd and OSIM International Pte Ltd, which included clause 5 on the composition of its Board of Directors.
Clause 5.1 of the 18 March 2011 Shareholders' Agreement stated that the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd would comprise three directors.

Clause 5.2.1 of the 18 March 2011 Shareholders' Agreement provided that two persons on the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd would be appointed by Paris Investment Pte Ltd and The Wellness Group Pte Ltd.

Clause 5.2.2 of the 18 March 2011 Shareholders' Agreement provided that one person on the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd would be appointed by OSIM International Pte Ltd, for so long as OSIM International Pte Ltd’s shareholding percentage was not less than 25%, and specified that this person would be Mr Ron Sim.

Following the 18 March 2011 sale and purchase transaction, the shareholdings in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd were 54.7% for The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, 35% for OSIM International Pte Ltd and 10.3% for Paris Investment Pte Ltd.

Clause 12 of the 18 March 2011 Shareholders' Agreement stated that, in the event of any inconsistency or conflict between the provisions of the agreement and the provisions of the articles of association of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, the provisions of the agreement would prevail as between the shareholders, applicable law, and that the shareholders would cause necessary alterations to be made to the articles to remove such conflict.

Article 91 of the constitution of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd provided that the Board of Directors could, at any time and from time to time, appoint any person to be a director, either to fill a casual vacancy or by way of addition to their number.

Following a rights issue conducted between 2013 and 2014, the shareholdings in TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd became 30.1% for The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, 58.6% for OSIM International Pte Ltd and 11.3% for Paris Investment Pte Ltd.

On 28 September 2012, director Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani resigned from the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, and no replacement director nominated by The Wellness Group Pte Ltd was appointed immediately thereafter.

As at 27 February 2017, the Board of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd comprised two directors: Mr Taha Bouqdib, appointed by OSIM International Pte Ltd, and Mr Ron Sim Chye Hock, appointed by Paris Investment Pte Ltd.

On 26 October 2016, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd received a proposal from The Wellness Group Pte Ltd to appoint Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani to its Board and, together with OSIM International Pte Ltd and Paris Investment Pte Ltd, responded that it would not appoint him and suggested alternative individuals.

On 13 February 2017, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd received a letter from The Wellness Group Pte Ltd proposing the appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen to its Board and requesting authorisation for him to share company information with The Wellness Group Pte Ltd and for him to be covered by director and officer insurance.

On 17 February 2017, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd received a further letter from The Wellness Group Pte Ltd requesting that it immediately take all necessary steps to formalise the appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen, including the requested ancillary arrangements.

On 21 February 2017, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd received a letter from The Wellness Group Pte Ltd demanding the formalisation of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen’s appointment as a director, without reference to the ancillary arrangements.

On 23 February 2017, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd replied to The Wellness Group Pte Ltd stating that it would not appoint Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen because the Board was unable to agree to the requested ancillary information and insurance arrangements and considered them not in the company’s interests.

On 27 February 2017, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd was named as a defendant in Originating Summons No 206 of 2017 filed by The Wellness Group Pte Ltd, which sought court orders relating to the appointment of a director to its Board.

On 17 July 2017, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd wrote to The Wellness Group Pte Ltd requesting written confirmation that the appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen was not contingent on the ancillary arrangements and stating that, upon receipt of such confirmation, it would consider the request to appoint him having regard to whether the appointment would be in the interests of the company.

On 21 July 2017, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd informed The Wellness Group Pte Ltd that its Board would convene a meeting as soon as practicable to consider the nomination of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen and stated that it disagreed that The Wellness Group Pte Ltd had an absolute and unfettered right to appoint any person it wished.
On 3 August 2017, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd informed The Wellness Group Pte Ltd that its Board would meet on 7 August 2017 to discuss the appointment of Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen.

On 8 August 2017, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd wrote to The Wellness Group Pte Ltd stating that the Board considered that Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen did not appear to have substantive commercial experience, particularly in the industry in which TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd operates, and requested an explanation of how his experience and qualifications were relevant and would add value to the Board.

Party_Details.Issue: How TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s constitutional power to appoint directors under Article 91 operates alongside the Shareholders' Agreement’s Board composition clause and the implied term concerning minority representation when The Wellness Group Pte Ltd nominates a director.
Party_Details.Rule: A company’s constitution may vest the formal power to appoint directors in the Board, while a shareholders’ agreement, to which the company is a party, can contractually constrain how that power is exercised by allocating Board seats among shareholders. Courts interpret such arrangements to give effect to both the constitutional appointment mechanism and the contractual allocation, so that the Board generally appoints the shareholder’s chosen nominee unless statutory disqualification or clear company-harm considerations apply.
Party_Details.Application: TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s constitution, through Article 91, authorises its Board to appoint directors to fill vacancies or increase the Board size. The 2011 Shareholders’ Agreement, to which TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd is a party, fixes the Board at three directors and allocates two seats to Paris Investment Pte Ltd and The Wellness Group Pte Ltd collectively and one seat to OSIM International Pte Ltd, with clause 12 providing that the agreement prevails over the constitution as between shareholders in case of conflict. After the 2013–2014 rights issue, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s shareholding structure changed, but the contractual Board allocation remained. When The Wellness Group Pte Ltd nominated first Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani and later Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen to fill its unoccupied Board seat, TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s Board responded by declining the first proposal, linking its refusal of the second proposal initially to ancillary information and insurance requests, and later requesting confirmations and further information about the nominee’s experience. These pre-dispute actions illustrate how TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s Board applied its constitutional appointment power in the context of the contractual Board seat allocation and the implied term concerning minority representation.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The Court examined TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s dual role as a party to the Shareholders’ Agreement and as the company whose constitution vested the formal power to appoint directors in its Board. It analysed Article 91 of the constitution, which authorises the Board to appoint directors, and clause 5 of the Shareholders’ Agreement, which fixes the Board size and allocates seats among the shareholders. The Court also considered clause 12 of the Shareholders’ Agreement, which states that the agreement prevails over the constitution as between the shareholders in the event of inconsistency, subject to applicable law, and obliges the shareholders to amend the constitution to remove conflicts.

In its reasoning, the Court held that the implied term, derived from earlier litigation, required that the minority shareholder holding at least 25% of the shares be entitled to one Board seat, while the majority shareholder(s) be entitled to two seats. It concluded that this implied term did not transfer the appointment power away from the Board but constrained how the Board must exercise that power: the Board is generally obliged to appoint the minority’s nominee unless the nominee is statutorily disqualified, does not consent to act, is obviously unfit for office, or would be obviously injurious to the company. The Court emphasised that the burden lies on the Board to demonstrate such unsuitability and that mere assertions about a nominee’s commercial experience or potential disagreement with existing directors are insufficient.

The Court rejected arguments that giving effect to the implied term would unlawfully usurp the Board’s powers, distinguishing authorities where shareholders in general meeting attempted to override management decisions vested in the board. It noted that all shareholders of TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd were parties to the Shareholders’ Agreement and had expressly agreed that its provisions would prevail over the constitution as between them. The Court relied on prior case law recognising the enforceability of shareholders’ agreements that regulate how powers conferred by the articles are to be exercised, and on decisions such as Golden Harvest, which upheld contractual arrangements concerning board governance even where the articles had not yet been amended.

Assessing TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s conduct, the Court reviewed the sequence of correspondence: the refusal to appoint Mr Manoj Mohan Murjani; the initial refusal to appoint Associate Professor Mak Yuen Teen linked to ancillary information and insurance requests; the subsequent requests for confirmations that the appointment was not contingent on those ancillary matters; and the later request for further information about the nominee’s experience. The Court concluded that TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd, acting through its Board, had not identified any statutory disqualification, lack of consent, obvious unfitness, or obvious injury to the company that would justify refusing the appointment. It held that the Board was not entitled to delay appointment by requiring The Wellness Group Pte Ltd to justify its choice and that, in the absence of a valid exception, the refusal to appoint the nominee constituted a breach of the implied term. Nonetheless, the Court dismissed the appeal on the specific relief sought, while clarifying the legal framework governing TWG Tea Company Pte Ltd’s Board appointment power in the presence of the Shareholders’ Agreement and the implied term.
